intro:
"Yoke-fellow" (Greek syzygos, literally "joined together") occurs once in Scripture, in Paul's appeal in Philippians 4:3: "I ask you also, true companion [syzygos], help these women, who have labored side by side with me in the gospel together with Clement and the rest of my fellow workers, whose names are in the book of life." The term evokes the image of two animals yoked together, pulling in the same direction—a vivid picture of collaborative gospel ministry and mutual dependence.

The identity of the unnamed "true yoke-fellow" has generated considerable speculation. Some have proposed that Syzygos is a proper name; others suggest Epaphroditus, Luke, Silas, or Timothy. Wycliffe rendered the phrase "the genuine fellow in yoke." Whoever is meant, Paul's use of the term is a mark of deep affection and shared burden: the yoke-fellow is not a subordinate assistant but a fellow laborer who carries the same load. The passage also indicates that women—Euodia and Syntyche—had been genuine partners in Paul's missionary work, their conflict now requiring pastoral intervention. The image of the gospel ministry as a shared yoke under Christ's easy burden (Matthew 11:30) underlies Paul's entire vision of the Christian community as people laboring together toward a common end.
